content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 14, 1993

Paula Treat
2948 Pasatiempo Place
Sacramento, CA 95833

Re: Your Letter Dated
May 16, 1993
Our File No. G-93-241

Dear Ms. Treat:

Thank you for your letter dated May 16, 1993, describing your
participation and that of your client, the California Correctional
Peace Officers Association ("CCPOA"), in the events held during
the week of March 22, 1993, to honor the late Assemblyman B. T.
Collins.

To summarize, your letter states that CCPOA paid $5,000 to
the Red Lion Inn and later reimbursed you $2,564.05 to purchase
hors d'oeuvres for a public "wake" held on March 25, 1993. You
estimated that 2,500 to 3,000 people, including many public
officials, attended the wake. Although there was a guest book
available, many attendees did not sign it. Your letter also
states that because you felt this was not a "political" event, the
payments were not disclosed on the lobbying disclosure reports
filed by you or CCPOA for the first quarter of 1993.

The purpose of this letter is merely to advise you that we
believe the costs of this event should be disclosed by you and
CCPOA as "activity expenses." Although we realize that the
payments were made to honor Assemblyman Collins and were not made
for the purpose of entertaining or making gifts to officials, the
Political Reform Act (Government Code Sections 81000, et seq.)
does not provide a method for exempting from disclosure payments
made by a lobbyist or lobbyist employer for an event at which
public officials receive benefits, such as food and beverages.

Normally, when reporting activity expenses, lobbyists and
lobbyist employers are required to itemize the name of each
reportable official who attended an event and the amount of
benefit attributable to each. However, the circumstances
surrounding this event were different from other types of events
at which officials receive benefits (i.e., there were
insufficient funds available from the family or other sources to
cover the costs of the event, there was insufficient time to
organize and solicit funds for the event, and the event was open

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Paula Treat
Our File No. G-93-241
Page 2


to the public).  In addition, the amount of benefit attributable
to each attendee appears to be de minimus (around $3.00).

     Therefore, under these unique circumstances, it is not
necessary for you or CCPOA to itemize the name of each reportable
official who attended the wake, but merely to provide a general
description of the event, the approximate number of attendees, and
the estimated cost per person.  On your lobbyist report (Form
615), you should also disclose that you paid $2,564.05 toward the
total cost of $7,564.05 and were reimbursed by CCPOA.  On its
lobbyist employer report (Form 635), CCPOA should separately
disclose the amount paid directly to the Red Lion Inn and the
amount reimbursed to you.  You may wish to attach a copy of this
letter to your amended reports to further explain the reason for
the amendments and the method of disclosure.

     We fully recognize and share the universal respect and
admiration that exists for the late Assemblyman B.T. Collins.  We
also appreciate that what was done in his honor was done as a
result of the genuine desire to pay tribute to a truly great
individual and state public official and not to intentionally
bestow a benefit on those who attended the memorial and wake.  It
is with this in mind that we offer the foregoing suggested
approach to you.

     We view this situation as highly unique and the advice set
forth herein is limited exclusively to the situation surrounding
the unexpected and unfortunate death of a great friend to the
State of California.  We sincerely regret the amount of time that
it has taken to provide a written response to your inquiry.  I
know you understand the extreme demand placed on all state
agencies in these times of reduced budgets, reduced staff and
increased workloads.  Again, thank you for providing this
information.  If you have any questions, please call Carla Wardlow
in the Technical Assistance Division at (916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Wayne Ordos
                                        Executive Director

sections.conclusion: Although we realize that the payments were made to honor Assemblyman Collins and were not made for the purpose of entertaining or making gifts to officials, the Political Reform Act (Government Code Sections 81000, et seq.) does not provide a method for exempting from disclosure payments made by a lobbyist or lobbyist employer for an event at which public officials receive benefits, such as food and beverages. However, under the unique circumstances of this event, it is not necessary to itemize each reportable official, but merely to provide a general description of the event, approximate number of attendees, and estimated cost per person. The lobbyist and lobbyist employer should separately disclose their respective portions of the total cost.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether costs paid by a lobbyist and lobbyist employer for a public memorial event honoring a deceased Assemblyman, at which public officials received food and beverages, must be disclosed as activity expenses under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the event costs must be disclosed as activity expenses, but given the unique circumstances—memorial nature, insufficient alternative funding, public attendance, and de minimis per-person benefit—itemization of individual officials is not required; instead, a general description with attendance count and per-person cost estimate is sufficient.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with no explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers; question and conclusion extracted from body text. Document date is June 14, 1993; file number G-93-241. De minimis benefit exception applied under unique circumstances.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether costs paid by a lobbyist and lobbyist employer for a public memorial event honoring a deceased Assemblyman, at which public officials received food and beverages, must be disclosed as activity expenses under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: Although we realize that the payments were made to honor Assemblyman Collins and were not made for the purpose of entertaining or making gifts to officials, the Political Reform Act (Government Code Sections 81000, et seq.) does not provide a method for exempting from disclosure payments made by a lobbyist or lobbyist employer for an event at which public officials receive benefits, such as food and beverages. However, under the unique circumstances of this event, it is not necessary to itemize each reportable official, but merely to provide a general description of the event, approximate number of attendees, and estimated cost per person. The lobbyist and lobbyist employer should separately disclose their respective portions of the total cost.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to Paula Treat regarding disclosure obligations for a $7,564.05 public memorial wake honoring Assemblyman B.T. Collins, concluding that the costs must be disclosed as activity expenses under the Political Reform Act but allowing simplified reporting given the event's unique circumstances.
